nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef drop-offs and outer reef slopes, generally at depths of 10–60 m

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on zooplankton (copepods, amphipods). Tends to accept frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp; has been known to transition to high-quality marine flake or pellet foods over time. An attached refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is generally advised to provide a steady live food supply, particularly during acclimation.

goodWith: other anthias species (with care during introduction)
avoidWith: predatory fish that eat small reef fish

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Debra Baker, 1660x1301. In-situ reef shot of Pseudanthias cooperi (kashiwae complex); whole fish in frame, reasonably sharp, pinkish-orange body visible. Best of the few commercial-friendly options; reds muted by blue underwater cast.
Photo by Debra Baker, 1738x1386. Second individual/angle showing purple dorsal margin and reddish pectoral fin; softer focus so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: The Red Fairy Anthias (Pseudanthias kashiwae) is a colorful, planktivorous reef fish from the Indo-Pacific, generally kept in small groups in spacious reef aquariums. Intermediate in care, it tends to do best with a regular supply of frozen or live foods and ample open water for swimming.
careNotes: Red Fairy Anthias are protogynous hermaphrodites — all individuals are born female, and in a group the dominant individual will transition to male. Males display a well-developed lateral red bar and a red caudal fin; females show red-tipped caudal fins and a pinkish-orange body. They have been known to jump from open aquaria, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is widely recommended to supplement frozen foods, especially during initial acclimation. The species is also sold under the name Silver-Streak Goldie Anthias.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Protogynous sequential hermaphrodites — the dominant individual in a haremic group transitions from female to male. In a well-maintained harem in a very large aquarium, spawning has been documented, but raising pelagic larvae through settlement is extremely difficult.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, tank size, care level, temperament, reef compatibility, and native range. Size disagreement: BlueZoo 4.5 in vs LiveAquaria 6 in — averaged to 11–15 cm range. Tank size disagreement: BlueZoo 70 gal (265 L) vs LiveAquaria 125 gal (473 L) — selected 500 L biasing toward larger given active swimming behavior and group housing requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: The Red Fairy Anthias is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite and is rarely bred in home aquaria. Spawning has been observed in large systems, but rearing the pelagic larvae through to settlement is extremely difficult.
